summary: Soundboard recording with hiss and mud but historically priceless; the early‑'67 Morning Dew and Viola Lee Blues shine.
review: The set captures a landmark moment – the Human Be‑In – and despite a noisy, hiss‑laden soundboard source the music still thrills.  Jerry’s guitar on “Morning Dew” (and the ensuing jam) is repeatedly called sublime, Phil lays down a solid bass foundation, and the raw power of “Viola Lee Blues” stands out, while the flute/rap on “Good Morning Little School‑Girl” divides listeners.  Audio quality is uneven – muddy at times, with hiss and occasional drop‑outs – but many fans feel the lo‑fi ambience adds to its nostalgic charm.

ai_rating.rationale: Average star rating across ~37 reviews is ≈4.0; most reviewers praise the performance (especially Dew and Viola Lee) while noting consistent recording flaws, yielding a solid but not exceptional overall score.

recording_quality.technical_notes: Noticeable tape hiss, occasional muddiness and drop‑outs; some describe it as “crisp enough” after EQ. The lo‑fi character is seen by many as adding nostalgic atmosphere, but pickier ears find it problematic.

show_quality.setlist_flow: Three‑track set from the Human Be‑In (Schoolgirl → Morning Dew → Viola Lee). Historically significant, raw and free‑form; some reviewers note the tracks are out of order on the release but the flow captures the psychedelic, communal vibe of the day.

band_member_comments.Jerry: Garcia’s guitar tone and solo work on “Morning Dew” and “Viola Lee Blues” receive high praise; a few note a minor mistake near the end of Dew.
band_member_comments.Phil: Lesh provides a solid bass foundation, especially in the closing jam; his occasional vocal cue (“slow down”) is mentioned. Some reviewers note his mix level is low.
band_member_comments.Keys: A flute (likely Charles Lloyd) appears on “Good Morning Little School Girl”; many find it distracting and out‑of‑key, though a few appreciate its psychedelic flavor.
band_member_comments.Drums: Bill Kreutzmann’s drumming is highlighted as powerful; there are mentions of a possible second drummer (Mickey Hart) on later portions, with mixed opinions about the timing.
